originalTitle: Thirty-five killed as gunmen attack Niger's biggest airport
title: Thirty-five killed as gunmen attack Niger's biggest airport
content: Niger has been fighting a militant Islamist insurgency for a decade and in January suspected jihadists attacked the same airport.

originalTitle: Lawyer in high-profile Ugandan treason case charged with related offence
title: Rules, Fairness, and Helping Our Friends in Uganda!
content:
Hi everyone! It's your kid-friendly news reporter here, bringing you super important news from our amazing continent, Africa! Today, we're going to talk about rules. Do you have rules at home? Like, no running inside? Or share your toys? Rules are super important, aren't they? Rules help us know what to do and how to get along. They make sure everything is fair and fun. Just like in your games, rules are super important in big countries too! Let's learn about a story from a beautiful country called Uganda.

Meet a Friend Who Helps People

In a country called Uganda, which is a beautiful part of East Africa, there are many people who work hard to make things fair for everyone. One of these people is named Erias Lukwago. He is like a helper-friend for grown-ups who might need someone to speak for them. Imagine you have a problem, and you need someone smart to explain your side. That's what Erias Lukwago does! He helps make sure everyone understands the rules and is treated fairly. He is like a referee in a big game, making sure everyone plays by the rules. He helps people understand their rights, which are like special protections that make sure everyone is treated with respect and fairness. Erias Lukwago is a grown-up who spends his time learning all about the big rulebook of the country to help others.

A Little Mix-Up with the Rules

Sometimes, even grown-ups can have big disagreements. Recently, Erias Lukwago had a big question about rules. He was asked to go and talk to some important people who work for the country. This is sometimes called being 'arrested,' which just means they wanted to ask him questions about something that happened. It's like when you and your friend have a disagreement about who took the last biscuit. A grown-up might ask both of you to explain what happened. That's what was happening with Erias Lukwago. They wanted to understand a situation better. It's important that when big questions like this come up, everyone remembers to follow the rules of fairness. Just like in a classroom, everyone should get a chance to explain their side of the story. This helps find the truth.

Why Rules Make Life Better

Let's think about why rules are so, so important. Imagine playing a game of soccer without any rules. Would it be fun? Probably not! Everyone would just kick the ball anywhere, and nobody would know what to do. Rules make games fair and fun. In our towns and countries, rules are like the instructions for how we all live together happily. They tell us what we can and cannot do. Rules help keep everyone safe and make sure everyone has a fair chance to live their best life. Without rules, things would be very confusing and maybe even a little bit scary! Did you know? Every country in Africa has its own special rules, called laws, that help people live together peacefully. These laws are like the big rulebook for the whole country! These laws help us know how to share, how to be kind, and how to solve problems without hurting anyone. That’s why following rules, and making sure rules are fair, is so important for all of us.

Special Helpers for Rules and Fairness

When there's a mix-up or a question about rules, we have special helpers. We have police officers who help keep everyone safe and find out what happened. And we have judges, who are like super-smart grown-ups who know all the rules in the big rulebook. Judges work in a special place called a court. A court is where people go to talk about disagreements and find out the truth. It's a very serious place, but it's all about making sure things are fair for everyone. Erias Lukwago helps people talk in court. Did you know? In many African countries, there are special 'village elders' or 'community leaders' who also help solve problems and make sure everyone in the village follows important traditions and rules! They are like local judges. Everyone, from the police to the judges, and helpers like Erias Lukwago, all work together to make sure that rules are followed and that everyone is treated fairly. They are like a big team for justice!

Understanding Big Words: "Charged"

Our news story mentioned that Erias Lukwago was 'charged.' That sounds like a big, complicated word, doesn't it? But it's actually simple! When someone is 'charged' with something, it just means that the important people in charge are saying, 'Hey, we think you might have done this thing that goes against a rule.' It's like if your teacher says, 'I charge you with taking the blue crayon without asking!' It doesn't mean you *definitely* did it, but it means they think you did, and now everyone needs to talk about it and find out the truth. So, when Erias Lukwago was 'charged,' it just means they told him what rule they thought he might have broken. Now, because of all the fairness rules, he gets to explain his side, and the smart judges will listen to everyone. It's all part of making sure things are fair.

Fairness is a Superpower for Everyone!

Fairness is so important! Imagine if in a game, only one team got to score points, and the other team never did. That wouldn't be fair, would it? And it wouldn't be fun! Fairness means everyone gets a chance to speak. Everyone gets to be heard. And everyone gets to be treated kindly, even when there are disagreements. In Uganda, just like in your home, school, and village, fairness makes everything better. When everyone tries to be fair, and when we all understand the rules, our communities become stronger and happier. We can learn from each other and grow together, just like a big family! Fairness helps us solve problems peacefully. Did you know? In many African cultures, people believe in 'Ubuntu,' which means 'I am because we are.' It's all about being kind, sharing, and treating everyone fairly, because we are all connected.

Working Together for a Happy Africa!

So, even when there are big questions and disagreements, we know that rules and fairness are there to help. People like Erias Lukwago help make sure that these important principles are followed. Our amazing continent of Africa is full of strong, kind people who want to live in peace and happiness. By understanding rules and always trying to be fair, we can all help build a wonderful future for Uganda and for all of Africa! Let's all remember to be fair in our games, fair with our friends, and fair in our hearts. Because fairness is a superpower that helps everyone shine! We can all be little helpers for fairness every day.
summary: A helper-friend in Uganda, Erias Lukwago, who works to ensure fairness and rules are followed, was asked questions by important people about a situation, and he will get to explain his side in a fair way.
whyMatters: This is important because it shows how rules and fairness help everyone in a country, making sure people can explain their side when there are big questions, which helps keep everyone safe and happy in Uganda and across Africa.

originalTitle: 'I buried my parents one day after the other' - Ebola mourners learn how to grieve safely
title: Staying Safe from Germs: How We Care for Each Other in Africa!
content:
Hello Friends! Your Kid Reporter Sunny Here!

Hi everyone! It's your friend Sunny here, your kid-friendly news reporter, bringing you important news from our amazing continent, Africa! Today, we're going to talk about something a little serious, but super important for keeping all of us, our families, and our friends safe and healthy. We're going to talk about a tiny germ called Ebola and how people are learning new, safe ways to say goodbye to loved ones when they pass away. It sounds tricky, but learning about it helps us stay strong and helps our communities get better!

What is Ebola? Meet the Tiny Germ!

Ebola is a tiny, tiny germ, so small you can't even see it! Imagine something much, much smaller than a grain of sand – that's how tiny germs are! These germs can make people very, very sick. It's like a bad flu, but much, much worse. When someone gets sick with Ebola, these little germs start to multiply inside their body. They make the person feel very hot (a fever), very tired, and can make their tummy hurt. **Did you know?** The Ebola germ got its name from a river in Africa, the Ebola River! Scientists found it there a long, long time ago. When someone has Ebola, these germs are inside their body and can be found on their skin and in their bodily fluids. If you touch someone who is sick with Ebola, or touch things they have touched, the germs can jump to you and make you sick too. This is why we have to be super careful and smart! But don't worry, there are lots of things we can do to stay safe.

Saying Goodbye in New, Safe Ways

Sometimes, even when someone dies from Ebola, the germs can still be on their body for a little while. In Africa, we have many special ways to say goodbye to people we love when they pass away. We call these funerals or burial ceremonies. A traditional funeral is usually a very big gathering. Everyone comes together. They sing, they cry, they hug each other. Sometimes, people even touch or wash the body of the person who has passed away. It is a very important way to show great love and respect for the person who has left us. But with Ebola, this way of saying goodbye can be very dangerous. Touching the body is like opening a door for the germs to spread. It's like inviting them to jump from the person who has passed to the people who are still alive. This means more people could get sick, and we don't want that! So, our smart doctors, brave health workers, and kind community leaders are teaching everyone new, safe ways to say goodbye. It's not easy because it's different from what we usually do, but it helps everyone else stay healthy and safe. It's a very brave thing for our communities to do.

What Are Safe Funerals?

Imagine our brave health workers, like superheroes, wearing special protective suits! They look like big raincoats, with gloves, masks, and goggles. These suits cover them from head to toe! These special clothes stop the germs from touching them. **Did you know?** These special suits are called Personal Protective Equipment, or PPE for short! They keep our helpers safe so they can help others without getting sick themselves. When someone dies from Ebola, these brave health workers carefully and gently prepare the person for burial. They make sure no one else gets sick by handling the body very carefully. Only these specially trained people can touch the body. Family members can still be there to say goodbye, but they have to stand a little far away. They can't hug or touch the body. This is very hard, we know, but it's super important to protect all the living family members and friends. And the burial usually happens quite quickly, often on the same day. This helps stop the germs from having time to spread to more people. It's a very different way to say goodbye, and it makes people sad because they can't have their usual big gatherings. But it's a very important way to show love by protecting everyone alive.

Grieving Our Loved Ones Safely

It's okay to feel sad, very, very sad, when someone you love dies. Even if you can't touch them or have a big party, your love for them is still there, and you can still remember them in your heart. How can we grieve safely? There are many ways: * You can talk to your mum or dad or another trusted grown-up about how you feel. It's good to share your feelings. * You can draw a picture to remember the happy times you had with that person. Maybe a picture of their smile or a fun day you spent together. * You can tell stories about them. Remembering their kind words, their funny jokes, or their special hug is a wonderful way to keep them in your heart. * You can write a letter to them, telling them all the things you miss. You can plant a small seed or a flower in their memory. * We can still show love to each other by being kind, by talking (from a safe distance if needed), and by supporting each other. A kind word or a helping hand (without touching!) can mean a lot. **Did you know?** Even when we are sad, remembering happy memories can help our hearts feel a little better, like sunshine on a cloudy day.

Our Busy Cemeteries

Because Ebola has made many people very sick, sadly, many people have passed away. This means that places where we bury people, called cemeteries, are getting very full. The cemeteries are places where we lay our loved ones to rest, to sleep peacefully. When many, many people pass away from the same sickness, these places fill up very, very fast. This is a very sad sign that we need to work even harder to stop the germs from spreading in our towns and villages. This is why safe funerals are not just a good idea, they are a *must* to keep our communities healthy and to save lives. Every time we follow the rules, like washing our hands or having a safe burial, we are helping to make sure fewer people get sick and our cemeteries don't get even fuller. It shows we care about our whole community and want to protect everyone.

What Kids Can Do to Help!

You, yes YOU, can be a superhero in this fight against germs! Here's how: * **Wash your hands!** This is the MOST important thing. Wash them often with soap and water, especially after playing, before eating, and after using the toilet. Sing your favourite song while you wash them for 20 seconds! That's how long it takes to make sure the germs are really gone. * **Don't touch sick people.** If someone looks very sick, keep a safe distance. Your grown-ups will know how to help them. * **Tell a grown-up!** If you feel sick or if you see someone else who looks very sick, tell a trusted adult right away. They will know what to do and how to get help. * Be kind to everyone. This is a tough time for many families, and being kind and understanding helps us all feel better and stronger together. **Did you know?** Washing your hands properly can get rid of many, many germs, not just Ebola! It's like magic for your health! Your hands might look clean, but only soap and water can truly chase those invisible germs away.

Hope for Tomorrow!

It might seem scary sometimes, but there is so much hope! Many smart doctors and scientists are working very, very hard to find more medicines to fight Ebola and even a special shot (a vaccine) to stop people from getting sick in the first place. Our communities in Africa are strong. We are learning new ways to stay safe and to care for each other, even when it's difficult. By listening to our elders and health workers, by being brave, and by washing our hands, we are helping Africa stay healthy and strong. Let's all work together to keep our families and friends safe. We can do it! We are brave, we are smart, and we care about each other. Until next time, stay safe and keep those hands clean!
summary: Kids in Africa are learning important new ways to say goodbye to family and friends safely during an Ebola outbreak, by changing traditional funerals and practicing good hygiene to stop germs from spreading.
whyMatters: It's important because by learning new ways to be safe and showing kindness, we can help stop the tiny Ebola germs from making more people sick, protecting our families and friends, and making our communities strong again.

originalTitle: Are prices really dropping in the US, as Trump claims?
title: Are prices really dropping in the US, as Trump claims?
content: Donald Trump boasts on social media that since signing of MoU for peace deal, oil is flowing and prices are dropping.

originalTitle: Cuba’s Communist Party approves opening economy in unprecedented move
title: Big News from a Sunny Island: Cuba Makes a Super Plan!
content:
Hello, my wonderful friends all across Africa! It's your friendly reporter here, bringing you exciting news from a faraway land. Today, we're going on an imaginary trip to a beautiful place called Cuba!

Hello from Your African Reporter!

I hope you are all having a super day, full of laughter and learning. I love sharing stories with you about our big, beautiful world. Sometimes, countries far away make big decisions that help their people. That's what's happening in Cuba! Did you know? Africa has many different countries, just like the world has many different countries! Each one is special.

Where in the World is Cuba?

Imagine a sunny island, a bit like some of the lovely islands we have close to Africa, but far away in another ocean. That's Cuba! It's surrounded by beautiful blue water. People there love music and dancing, and they have very friendly smiles. Did you know? An island is a piece of land that has water all around it! It's like a small boat floating in a big bathtub.

Who Makes the Rules in Cuba?

Every country has grown-ups who help make important decisions for everyone. In Cuba, there's a special group called the "Communist Party." Think of them as the leaders who help decide how the country works and how people can get what they need. They have been making all the big plans for a very, very long time. But now, they have made a *new* big plan!

What Does "Opening Up" Mean?

This is the most exciting part! The leaders in Cuba have decided to "open up" their economy. What does that mean? It means they are letting more people start their own small businesses! Yay! Imagine your mum or dad wanting to bake delicious cakes to sell, or fix bicycles, or grow yummy vegetables to share with neighbours. Before, it was a bit harder for people to do these things on their own. The government usually did most of these jobs. Now, more people can be like small shop owners! They can open little shops, cook food to sell, or make things like clothes and toys. This is a very big change for Cuba. Did you know? "Economy" is a big word that just means how people in a country make money and get all the things they need, like food, clothes, and school books!

Why Are They Doing This Now?

Sometimes, even in lovely sunny places, countries can have hard times. For a while, it has been a bit tricky for the people in Cuba to get all the things they need. It's like when your favourite shop runs out of your best snack. So, the leaders decided they needed to do something special, and do it quickly! This new plan is like a helping hand for the whole country. They hope it will bring more food, more clothes, and more happy things for everyone. Did you know? When things are tricky, people often come up with new, smart ideas to help each other!

What Made Things Tricky?

Well, there's another very big country, far away, called the United States of America (USA). For a very long time, the USA has had some rules that make it difficult for Cuba to buy and sell things with other countries. This is called a "pressure campaign." It's a bit like if some friends at school had a rule not to share their lunch with another friend. It would make it hard for that friend to get enough food, wouldn't it? This made it hard for Cuba to get things like special machines, medicines, or even enough food sometimes. Because of these rules, Cuba has been struggling, which means things have been very, very difficult for the people living there. It's like trying to run a race with your shoelaces tied together. Did you know? Countries often buy and sell things to each other. This is called "trade." It helps everyone get what they need and want!

Hoping for Sunshine in Cuba!

So, this new plan to let people start their own small businesses is very important. It means more people can work and earn money, and there will be more things for everyone to buy and use. It's like planting many small seeds that will grow into big, healthy plants! The leaders in Cuba are hoping that this new idea will help their country get stronger and happier. They want to make sure everyone has enough and can live a good life. And from here in Africa, we send our best wishes to our friends in Cuba! We know that working together and trying new things can make a big difference for everyone. It's exciting to see a country make such a brave and hopeful change! Did you know? Even small changes can sometimes lead to very big and wonderful improvements for many people! Just like a tiny seed can grow into a giant tree. I'll be back soon with more fun news from around our amazing world. Bye for now!

originalTitle: Myanmar military airstrike kills seven civilians in Rakhine state
title: Myanmar military airstrike kills seven civilians in Rakhine state
content: A Myanmar military airstrike on the Rakhine state town of Kyauktaw killed at least seven civilians.

originalTitle: US lifts naval blockade as Iran's supreme leader says Trump made deal 'out of desperation'
title: Big News! Ships Can Sail Free Again!
content:
Hello, Amazing Friends!

Hello, wonderful friends! It's your friendly news reporter for Africa, ready to share some big news from far away! Imagine two big countries, like two big families living very far from each other. Sometimes, families don't always agree, right? Well, these two big countries, America and Iran, had a little problem. But guess what? Something good just happened!

What is a Blockade?

First, let's learn a big word: 'blockade'. Can you say it? Block-aid! Good job! Imagine a big, big fence. But this fence is not on land. It's in the ocean! It's made of lots of big ships. These ships stop other ships from going in or out of a place. It's like putting a 'stop sign' in the sea. For a while, no ships could carry toys, food, or other important things to or from Iran. This blockade made it hard for people to get what they needed. Did you know? The ocean is huge! It covers most of our planet. Ships help us send things all around the world, like yummy fruits from one country to another!

Why Was There a Problem?

So, why did America put up this big ocean fence for Iran? Well, countries sometimes have disagreements. Maybe they don't agree on how to do things, or they worry about what the other country is doing. It's like when you and your friend might disagree on which game to play. These big countries had very serious disagreements that made them stop talking nicely for a while. The blockade was a way for America to say, 'We are not happy with what's happening!' Did you know? Leaders of countries are like the head of a very big family. They try to make the best decisions for all the people in their country.

Making a Deal: Like Making Friends Again!

But here's the happy part! When people have problems, what's the best thing to do? Talk! Yes! America and Iran started talking to each other. And they made a 'deal'. A deal is like making a promise. It's when two sides agree to do certain things to solve their problem. Think of it like this: you and your friend had a fight over a toy. Then you talk and agree, 'Okay, I will share the toy if you let me play first.' That's a deal! Making a deal helps everyone feel better. Did you know? When countries talk to solve problems, it's called 'diplomacy'. It's a fancy word for being good at talking and finding solutions!

The Big News: The Fence is Gone!

Now, the really big news! America decided to take down its big ocean fence! Yes! The naval blockade is lifted. This means the big ships can sail freely again! Ships can carry food, clothes, toys, and all sorts of important things to and from Iran. This is a very good step because it means the countries are trying to be friends again. It helps people get what they need and can make the world a more peaceful place. Did you know? The biggest ships in the world are called 'container ships'. They are like giant floating warehouses, carrying thousands of boxes!

One Leader Isn't So Sure

Now, remember we said a deal is like a promise? Well, sometimes, even after a promise is made, not everyone is super happy about it. The leader of Iran, who is a very important person there, said he thinks America's old president, Mr. Trump, made this deal because he was 'desperate'. Desperate means really, really wanting something to happen, maybe because things were not going well for him. So, Iran's leader doesn't quite agree with *why* the deal was made, even if the big ocean fence is now gone. Did you know? Different countries have different kinds of leaders. Some are called presidents, some are prime ministers, and some are supreme leaders, like in Iran!

Why Is This Important for Our World?

This news is very important, not just for America and Iran, but for everyone, everywhere! When big countries have problems, it can make things difficult for many other countries too. When they make a deal and take down big ocean fences, it helps bring peace. And peace is wonderful! It means people can live, work, and play without worrying. It helps everyone on our big, beautiful planet be safer and happier. Did you know? When countries work together, it's like a big team. Teams can do amazing things, like build new schools or find ways to share water!

What Does This Mean for You?

You might be thinking, 'I live in Africa, what does this mean for me?' Well, even though these countries are far away, we are all part of one big world family. When there is peace in one part of the world, it helps make the whole world a better place. It shows us that even big problems can be solved if people talk and try to find solutions. And that's a lesson we can all learn, whether we are big countries or just friends on the playground! Did you know? Africa is a huge continent with many amazing countries and cultures. We are all connected!

Let's Learn Together!

So, remember this big news! America took down its big ocean fence. Ships can sail freely again. And countries are trying to make things better, even if they don't always agree on everything. It's a big step towards a more peaceful world. Keep asking questions and learning about our amazing world. You are all incredible news detectives!
summary: America lifted a big ocean fence, letting ships sail freely again, even though Iran's leader has mixed feelings about the deal that was made.
whyMatters: This is important because when big countries make peace, it helps everyone in the world be safer and happier, making it easier for important goods to travel everywhere.
